title: Tag Up: Reading Depth and Timing the Break

root.narration: Bottom of the 7th, you're on third base with one out, down 4-3. The batter lofts a fly ball to medium-depth right field. It's not deep -- the right fielder is camped under it about 280 feet from home plate. A truly deep fly ball (320+ feet) is an easy tag. A shallow fly ball (250 feet or less) is a clear hold. This one is in the gray zone. The right fielder has the strongest arm on their team. You need to decide: is it deep enough to tag up and score? You have about 3 seconds while the ball is in the air to make this decision.

choices.text: Tag and go! It's medium depth -- if I get a good jump the moment the ball is caught, I can beat the throw. The tying run is worth the risk.
Hold. The right fielder has the best arm on the team and this fly ball isn't deep enough. 280 feet means his throw is only about 250 feet to home. Too risky.

Tag the base and take two aggressive steps toward home -- bluff the run. If the right fielder throws home, the batter might advance to second on the throw. If he throws to the cutoff instead, I stay at third.

decision-tag-2.narration: You decide to tag up! You put your foot on the bag and watch the right fielder settle under the fly ball. Everything depends on your TIMING now. Do you push off the bag the instant the ball hits the glove, or do you leave a split second early -- technically illegal but hard to catch -- to get a bigger head start?

choices.text: Leave the INSTANT the ball hits the leather. Train my eyes on the ball, and the moment I see the glove close, I explode. Legal, clean, maximum jump within the rules.
Leave a hair early -- just before the catch. The umpire is 90 feet away and I'll time it so it looks simultaneous. Every millisecond matters on a medium-depth fly.

decision-perfect-throw.narration: You leave the instant the ball hits the glove -- perfect timing! You explode down the line toward home. The right fielder catches the ball and fires a throw home. It's going to be close. You're sprinting at full speed and the ball is in the air on a line toward home plate. You're 30 feet from home when the ball is 40 feet from the catcher. It's going to be a BANG-BANG play. How do you slide?

choices.text: Hook slide! Slide to the back of the plate, away from the catcher. Make him reach across his body to tag me. Even if the throw beats me, the tag has to travel farther.
Slide straight in. Get to the plate as fast as possible. A straight slide covers the shortest distance from my current position to the plate.

outcome-hold-okay.narration: You hold at third. The right fielder catches the ball and throws it back to the infield. No play at the plate. Two outs, runner on third. The next batter strikes out -- inning over. You're still trailing 4-3. The right fielder's arm was strong, but his throw went to the cutoff man, not home. If you had tagged up, the throw wouldn't have been directly to the plate. There's no way to know for sure if you would have been safe, but the opportunity to tie the game is gone. Sometimes the conservative play costs you the run you needed.

outcome.headline: Held at Third. Tying Run Stranded.
outcome.explanation: The hold at third isn't wrong from a pure risk-avoidance perspective. If you're thrown out at the plate, that's the second out and the inning continues with nobody in scoring position. But in a down-one game in the late innings, the COST of not scoring the tying run is extremely high. The next batter struck out, meaning the run never scored. Every tag-up decision in the late innings should weight the VALUE of the run against the RISK of the out. In a tie game or down-one scenario, the value of the tying run is enormous. You need a very high probability of being thrown out (70%+) to justify holding. On medium-depth flies (280 feet), the tag-up success rate is approximately 60-65% even against strong arms.
outcome.whatToRemember: In late innings when trailing by one, be aggressive on tag-ups from third. The value of the tying run is so high that you should tag unless the probability of being thrown out is very high (70%+). Medium-depth flies are usually worth the attempt.

outcome-bluff-good.narration: You tag and take two aggressive steps toward home -- then stop! The right fielder sees you going and panics. He catches the ball and throws directly home instead of hitting the cutoff man. The throw sails to the catcher -- nobody is there to score. But the batter, who reached first on the putout, sees the throw going home and takes off for second base! He makes it to second on the unnecessary throw home. Now you're at third and the batter is at second with two outs. The bluff created an extra base for the offense even though you didn't score.

outcome.headline: Bluff Tag Creates Extra Base! Smart Baserunning.
outcome.explanation: The bluff tag is an advanced baserunning tactic that exploits the outfielder's decision-making. When you aggressively break toward home, the outfielder's instinct is to throw to the plate. But if the throw goes home and you retreat to third, the batter can advance. This only works when: 1) You sell the bluff convincingly (aggressive first steps). 2) You stop in time to get back to third safely. 3) The batter is aware of the play and ready to advance. The bluff doesn't score the run, but it improves your base-out state by putting another runner in scoring position. With two outs and runners on second and third, a single ties the game. Without the bluff, the batter is still on first and needs a double or more to score both runners.
outcome.whatToRemember: The bluff tag from third creates defensive confusion and can advance other runners. Even when you can't score, an aggressive bluff forces the outfielder to make a throw to the plate, opening up bases for trailing runners.

outcome-early-leave-bad.narration: You leave a hair early -- just before the catch. You sprint home and slide in ahead of the throw! The catcher tries to tag you but you're already touching the plate! SAFE! Wait -- the right fielder's team is appealing! They throw the ball to third base and the umpire signals... you left EARLY! You're OUT on the appeal! The run doesn't count! You would have been safe on the play, but leaving early turned a tying run into an appeal out. The third out of the inning. Game over.

outcome.headline: Appeal Out! Left Early and Lost the Run!
outcome.explanation: Leaving early on a tag play is one of the most devastating mistakes in baseball because the defense can appeal it AFTER the play. Even if you slide in safe at home, the run is erased if you left the base before the catch. The appeal play goes like this: the defense throws to third base and the umpire rules whether you left early. If you did, you're out and the run doesn't count. The extra 50 milliseconds you gained by leaving early was WORTHLESS because the play at the plate was already close enough to be safe with a legal jump. Elite baserunners train themselves to leave on the SOUND of the ball hitting the glove -- that way they're gone the instant the catch is made, with zero risk of leaving early. Eyes on the ball, ears on the glove, feet on the bag. The moment the sound registers, EXPLODE.
outcome.whatToRemember: NEVER leave early on a tag play. The appeal out erases the run and costs you the out. Train to leave on the SOUND of the catch -- eyes see the ball approaching, ears confirm the catch, feet push off simultaneously.

outcome-hook-slide-great.narration: You hook-slide to the back of the plate, away from the catcher! The throw arrives at the same time you do -- BANG-BANG! The catcher catches the ball and swipes his glove toward you... but you're on the BACK SIDE of the plate! His tag sweeps through empty air where a straight slider would have been! Your hand reaches out and touches the back corner of the plate. SAFE! TIE GAME! The hook slide added 6 inches of distance to your slide, but it moved the TAG point 18 inches farther from the catcher. The net result: the catcher had to reach across his body to make the tag, and he couldn't get there in time.

outcome.headline: HOOK SLIDE SAFE! Tying Run Scores on a Tag Up!
outcome.explanation: The hook slide is the highest-IQ slide on a close play at the plate. Here's why: a straight slide brings you directly to the catcher, who is set up on the front side of the plate. The tag is easy because you're sliding into his glove. A hook slide takes you AROUND the catcher to the back of the plate. Now the catcher has to catch the ball AND reach across his body to apply the tag. This forces him to do two things simultaneously instead of one: receive the throw AND redirect the tag. On a bang-bang play, the extra 0.2 seconds the catcher needs to redirect his tag is often the difference between safe and out. The hook slide sacrifices some speed (slightly longer path) for a massive tag-avoidance advantage.
outcome.whatToRemember: On close plays at the plate, the hook slide beats the straight slide. It moves the tag point away from the catcher, forcing him to reach across his body. The tag-avoidance advantage outweighs the slightly longer sliding path.

keyTerms.definition: A slide where the runner curves away from the fielder to reach the back or side of the base, making the tag harder to apply. Sacrifices a fraction of speed for significant tag avoidance.
The location where the fielder must apply the tag on the runner. Hook slides move this point away from the fielder's natural position, requiring extra reach and time.
A play where the runner and the throw arrive at nearly the same time. On these plays, the slide type and tag application become the deciding factors, not speed or arm strength.

outcome-straight-slide-good.narration: You slide straight in! The throw arrives and the catcher catches it right in front of the plate. His glove comes down and meets you on the front edge of the plate. The tag and your slide hit the plate at the exact same time. The umpire pauses... OUT! The catcher got the tag down a fraction of a second before your hand touched the plate. It was the closest play of the game. A hook slide to the back of the plate would have moved the tag point 18 inches away from the catcher and given you the margin you needed. The straight slide was the fastest route but the easiest to tag.

outcome.headline: OUT by a Fraction! Straight Slide Into the Tag.
outcome.explanation: The straight slide covers the shortest distance but brings you directly into the catcher's glove. On a play this close, the slide type is the deciding factor. The straight slide arrival time was actually FASTER than a hook slide would have been -- but the tag arrival time was also faster because the catcher didn't have to reach. The hook slide would have been 0.1 seconds slower in arrival but 0.3 seconds slower for the tag, creating a net 0.2-second advantage. In baseball, 0.2 seconds at the plate is about 5 inches. That's the difference between safe and out. On bang-bang plays at the plate, tag avoidance matters more than pure speed.
outcome.whatToRemember: On close plays at the plate, the hook slide is better than the straight slide even though it covers more distance. The tag avoidance advantage (making the catcher reach) outweighs the slight speed loss.
